speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape nine with Mrs. Rachel Warszauer Schlufman, And today is August 29, 1995. Mrs. Schlufman, you were telling me about your children and how proud you are of them and how they've been successful in the United States. Can you tell me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] why it's been important for you to do this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why it's important-- because I'm a survivor. I'm the witness. And we are people-- how long? How many years do we have? And I feel it's our obligation to tell and to retell like Passover, our history. It's only by mouth to retell. If we tell, it should stay with generation to generation. It should never be forgotten. The Holocaust was the most horrible, horrible event that took place in the world that so many millions, six million Jews, 50 million people were gone for what reason?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the young generation should know and should learn. This history should be the most important history to learn about what happened those years. When we are gone, who will tell? So I'm very happy and I'm proud, and I bless Mr. Spielberg for doing this job. God bless him. And God bless all the Jewish organizations who helped to organize this kind of work to prevent from having another Holocaust. I know that you've done some writing. I'd like you to tell me a little bit how you got involved in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the truth is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a member in the Hadassah. When I came to America, I went to school at night. I wanted to be a part [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while I live in America, not to be backwards. I wanted to know how to write. I wanted to-- to be able to read the paper. It's just it was in me, you know? And I became a  member in the Hadassah. That goes back already-- I'm a member already about, oh, 20 years-- more than that, a member. And I started to write little stories about my life, about the Holocaust, of course. And the chair lady from our chapter was very impressed what I'm writing. But of course, she felt a little bit-- you know, I-- I looked at her, and she says, it's very sad, but you-- but you're writing very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she placed all these little stories in the bulletin, which I was a little embarrassed, you know? I says, I am not writing-- I'm just showing you because you are the chair lady. Just give me your opinion if I write. But she says, you know, Rachel? I think that you have a knack for writing. And go on. Do it. I love the way you write. And women started to call me up with the chapter. They read my article. They liked it so much. But what I'm going to do? She said, take all these pages and pile them up. You will see sometimes you go-- you will be able to publish a book. I says, what? A book? What-- she says, yes. You definitely have a feeling for writing, and I like the way you express yourself. You're telling it very clearly. I can understand you. And it's very touchy, of course, for us, but it's important for us to listen what people went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not every survivor can express themselves. They're hiding it. They don't want to tell. But if you are willing, give it out. I says, yes, I would like to give it out. And that's when I started to write page by page. And then she sent me to a-- a publisher. I wasn't, you know-- I wasn't successful. It took me a while, a couple of years, until it was accepted. And-- and I started to-- I started to-- before I started to write, of course, I went to school, and I took up writing. I took up special writing, you know. And that helped me a lot too in spelling and writing. I looked up in the dictionary-- when I was writing, my eyes went blind. I looked up in the dictionary constantly picking up the words how to spell. It wasn't easy for me. But everything what you have in my book, when you read my book, all these stories were written from my heart with my hands. Of course, my editor did a little touch up on it, which every book, I assume, needs it. But I-- I'm very happy I did it. I gave out the pain. I have the names for my dearest.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I didn't feel good a few years ago, I was in the hospital for a-- for a minor little thing. I took my book, and I held it on my chest. When the doctor came in, and he says, what is that? I turned the book, and I showed him my picture. He says, it's you? I says, yes. Well, I give you a lot of credit. Took the book, and he started, he's, like, let me show it to the other doctors. He says, look-- look who we have here. I said, you haven't got nobody. You have a survivor here who gave out her pain. I assume you wouldn't like to read that. He says, don't say that. As a matter of fact, I gave the book for his wife to read. But I'm writing now the story and I hope that I will finish. I love to write, you know? It's very interesting. It's lively stories. It has a lot of humor in it, but also explains for the young generation that America, from the beginning, wasn't easy for surviving, you know, especial for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years-- for many-- you know how luck-- how you pick-- you-- how a person has their luck. The first 10 years was very difficult with the language, when a child got sick, with money-wise, you know, with an apartment, and with Dad, and with his job, in and out. It wasn't easy. It was a hassle. We, you know-- it-- it-- it was very, very difficult. But with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, when you get older, somehow, you know, the years, you know-- I-- I did see that life is not-- not so easy. But I started to help my husband. I worked a little bit. That helped, too, to send my children to a yeshiva, which I wanted. I wanted to give them that education. I felt for men, it's very important to have that. Education you cannot have when you are little boys. You have to give them when they're young. And that stays with you the way I had it. And I wanted to give it to my children. I think it's important. You cannot buy this for money. It stays with you. And so that's what I did. And I'm very proud of myself that I helped out my husband. I worked. And after many-- after 20-- 20 years of working, I said to myself that's enough whatever I did. I have to do something with myself. I took a turn point in my life. I says-- I went back to school, and I-- and I took some courses, starting to write a life in America, some poetry, and paintings, which I enjoy very, very much in my spare time. When I get tired with writing, I can sit for hours and paint.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm very busy lady-- lady in my-- in my age, in my retirement. I keep this, and I keep that. And I just hope that God grants me healthy years to go on with my life together with my husband, my children. And that's all that I can tell you-- just to be well in my age. I hope so. I would like you to read a poem that you wrote for your-- in memory of your nieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: My nieces were very dear to me, very dear. My sister very-- spoke very little about them. And I wrote that poem mainly. She spoke very little about them because it was very-- she didn't want to hurt her children, you know, her other children who-- with whom, you know, they were born after the war. She didn't want to bring so much pain to them. But when she came to my house, she never stopped talking about them. And because my sister's gone now, and her husband, too, there's nobody to mention about the past, especially about little girls, little children, which I love, especially girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'To my nieces in their nest. My dearest little nieces, it is so nearly 50 years since you have been seen or heard. But in my heart, you are still riding [BACKGROUND NOISE] on a life merrry-go-round. Would that the ponies would carry you around again so we could play again, as we did before the flames took our rights, our parks, our homes, our families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How innocent you were to be guilty of death with millions of other children who were victims of human inhumanity. Weren't children born to live, to feel, and give our greatest joy? The adults in jackboots must never have been children themselves. My nieces, you were such a loud joy. Yet, you were incredibly brave and wise not to add to your parents' suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: As momma and papa birds lost all wings and feathers, and all they could provide you birdlings was a little water, a few crumbs, and much faith. When they get [INAUDIBLE], you quickly run out of food, water, then hop in your bunker. Papa first emerged from the hiding nest with Rachel, his oldest, hand in hand. The helmet hawks were hiding, too. They pounced and killed your father, leaving you, my namesake niece, to run over the blood-smeared rubble. You courageously find your way back to Mama in the hiding nest. You begged-- you hugged her as she nestled into her grief, knowing that each kiss, and each tear, and your injured head could be the last before you, too, would go join your papa in God's hidings nest. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I'm grateful for the miracle of God granted me, sparing my life and allowing me to live in America. We came to America in 1950 from Augsburg, Germany with the belief that we could pursue a better life in a free country. My only hope is that we continue to remember our holy people-- how they lived and how would they die. If you forget the Nazi atrocities, the future is sure to again be ablaze with the Holocaust. Every book, every tape, every film on the Holocaust is a hedge against future destruction. Together we will carry the torch symbolizing the pain of those innocent victims who perished for the only sin of belonging to the Jewish race.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you so much for doing this film. I will always remember you. And God bless you all. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Please tell us who is in the picture here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. It's me, Rachel Schlufman, now. I was Rachela [INAUDIBLE] Warszauer then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? I ?] was taken in Lodz in [INAUDIBLE] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year of-- the beginning of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second marriage-- my sister's second marriage to Joel. This was in Augsburg with her son Shlomele in the year of on January in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Tobcha-- this is Tobcha your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's my sister Tobcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Joel's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel's Morgenstern. Joel Morgenstern-- from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my family. This is Joel. This is my sister's husband. This is Tobcha with her little son Shlomo, Shlomele. And that's my husband Herb. And that's me with my son, Shlomele. That was taken-- that picture was taken in Augsburg, in springtime, the year of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, tell me from the left side who that first person on the left is.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, Herb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the left, that's Tobcha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tobcha. This is Tobcha, my sister Tobcha

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then standing next to her is Joel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the right side with your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yes. No, the left side. This side is me and Herb. The right side is Joel and  Tobcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so let's start again. From the right side, that's Joel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel and Tobcha with her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And on the left side?

words.text: OK. And on the left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the left side is me with Herb with my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm sorry. I mixed it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. Right, right. His name is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my sister's new husband with her two children. The third-- this is her third husband. She got married in Israel. The children are from Joel, from her second husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the daughter's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: The daughter's name is Chajale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband's name was Abram-- Abram and Shlomele.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Abram's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eisenreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this was taken in Israel, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they were married in Israel before they-- they came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to America [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- in the '60s-- in 1961-- around-- 1960, around that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from Israel. From the-- from the right, it's me, and that's my husband. And that picture of was taken in 1949 before we left to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Augsburg. Augsburg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my brother, Shmulek, which we call him now Sam. And that's my sister-in-law Elaine. She's born in England, a Manchester girl. And they live in Manchester. They have three wonderful sons. That's my brother with Elaine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam's last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name, Warszauer Sam Warszauer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Manchester.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they got married. I wouldn't tell you exactly. I don't remember exactly what date. But you know, I didn't have a chance to call them up. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, 1960, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me with my husband Herb. This is our 25th anniversary-- that picture in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my older son, Solomon, with his wife Janet. That's me. That's my husband-- my youngest son Joseph. That's my family. God bless you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Their wedding was in June 24, the year of 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother Sam with his wife Elaine and little-- Jason, the youngest son. And on the top on the left-- on the right, I mean, you see my nephew, Shlomele, when you saw the pictures before with his wife Gita. That's me in the middle with my husband Herb. On the left side, you will see Regina, my sister-in-law with her husband, Harry. And this is cousins-- my husband's cousins-- Abe [? Wasserman ?] with Dora. She's the one, we survived together in Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this is all your son's wedding, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son's wedding. That's the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's the whole family. This is my husband, Herb. We are married, it's going to be 50 years in 1996 on January 2. God bless you, and I love you all these years. You promised me health and money, [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH], but love forever. We should only be well. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and I love you dear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, dear.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me courage to go on, to write, to keep up with my life, to be busy, and I thank you. And I appreciate to have you for a husband. And I want to keep you for many, many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. So do I.
